thread.subject: made Strive Soda something
thread.body_text:
Christopher, saw strive's angle pairs a quarterly giveaway with 15% off to hook first orders, curious how that's been converting.

 separate note: I made Strive Soda a 4-min ad fatigue report from your live meta ads. the one about to die, the duplicate eating budget, which to scale, and a fresh angle to test.

 most brands in your niche usually run the same handful of ads too long. this spots it before ROAS drops.

 want it?

 Kyle H.
 Co-founder | OmniRocket

 P.S. if Jack's the better person, point me there :)

Kyle - I get 10 of these emails a day so please send me whatever you think is valuable for Strive and I’ll take a look.
 

 - CC
 CHRIS CLARK, MBA
 Head Of Marketing 
 +1-925-683-9911 
 chris@strivesoda.com 
 Website   |   Instagram  |  LinkedIn

here's the report Christopher - Strive Soda's 4-min fatigue report. 

 it flags the ad about to stop working, the near-duplicate splitting your budget, the one to put more behind, and an angle you're not running yet.

 the fixes are quick but easier to just walk you through - 30 min? → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: made Strive Soda something
thread.body_text:
Kyle - thanks for sending this over. I'll booked a time for Monday.

Warmly,
Chris

Awesome - looking forward to our call on Monday. 

 Have a great weekend.

 Best,
 Kyle

copy.tldr: Strive Soda is a San Diego better-for-you hydration soda whose Meta ads are running near-duplicate copy and making a claim their own site contradicts, and this call is your chance to show Chris the fix and a clean 30 day test.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A San Diego direct-to-consumer drink brand. They call themselves the first hydration soda: a sparkling drink with electrolytes and organic honey, sold in 12-packs. Founder is Jack Espy. Your contact is Chris Clark, Head of Marketing.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Three products, all $32.99 for a 12-pack: Peach Perfect, Tropical Recharge, and a Variety Pack with 6 of each. Every can has 180mg electrolytes, is sweetened with organic wildflower honey, and is 45 calories. They also push a Subscribe & Save first month at $16.50.
deep_dive.positioning: Premium and playful better-for-you soda. Bold flavor first, hydration second. Not a gym or performance drink, not a sugar bomb. Made for active, on-the-go adults who want cleaner soda without giving up taste.
deep_dive.who_buys: Active adults who love soda but want cleaner ingredients and everyday hydration, and will pay a premium to get both. Think beach-to-boardroom California lifestyle.
strengths: Clear, differentiated hook: soda first, hydration built in, sweetened with honey not sugar. That is easy to say in an ad.
Real offer ladder already in place: Variety Pack to try both flavors, plus a strong Subscribe & Save first-month price of $16.50.
gaps: Their live ads say '0 sugar, 0 calories' but their own site says the soda is sweetened with honey and is 45 calories per can. That is a contradiction that will hurt trust and could get flagged.
No reviews are visible on the site except small counts (2 on Peach, 0 on Tropical, 14 on Variety). Little visible social proof makes paid ads harder to convert.

game_plan.headline: A 30 day test to find the one angle and one audience that sells Strive, starting by fixing the ads that are fighting each other.

phases.goal: Find which message and which audience actually make people buy. This is a learning phase, not a scaling phase.
campaigns: One clean testing campaign built around three customer avatars (below), each with its own angle. We stop running near-duplicate ads against each other so the budget stops splitting.
A simple retargeting campaign for people who visited the site or added to cart, leaning on the Variety Pack and the Subscribe & Save first-month price.
lead_products: Variety Pack (12 Pack) as the main lead. It has the most reviews (14 vs 2 and 0), which tells me it is the one people pick most, and it lets a first-time buyer try both flavors so there is less risk in the first order.
Peach Perfect (12 pack) as the flavor-led backup. It has a couple of reviews and the strongest single-flavor story ('a little slice of paradise').
phases.creative: Three angles, one per avatar. Angle 1 (the soda swap): 'soda you can drink every day' for the soda lover who wants cleaner. Angle 2 (the afternoon slump): 'light fizz plus hydration when water feels dull and soda feels like too much' for the busy on-the-go adult, built off their own 'gets me through the day' ad. Angle 3 (the clean-label taste): honey-sweetened, 45 calories, no artificial sweeteners, for the ingredient reader. Every ad must match the site: honey-sweetened and 45 calories, NOT '0 sugar 0 calories'. Mix static cans-on-beach visuals with simple founder or talking-to-camera style video.
phases.budget: Start around $150 a day across the testing campaign, with a small slice held for retargeting. Hold this steady for the full 30 days so each angle gets a fair read before we judge it.
phases.what_success_looks_like: By day 30 we can name the one angle and one audience that bought best, we have killed the losers, and retargeting is quietly picking up the visitors the cold ads warmed up. Clarity on what to scale, not scale itself.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this drinks category, which is the floor for learning which angle works without waiting months.

estimates.label: Average order value we planned around
estimates.basis: Every product on the site is priced at $32.99, so we used that single price as the stand-in for average order value. If most orders are subscriptions at $16.50 or bundles of several packs, this changes and we need to hear the real number from Chris.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a dollar figure on 30 days
estimates.basis: 30 days is a learning window and we cannot see their current sales, spend, or return on ad spend. A revenue promise here would be a guess. Once we know their real average order value and repeat rate, we can size this honestly.

assumptions: Average order value is near the $32.99 single-pack price. If it is really the $16.50 subscription price, the math for paid ads gets tighter and we should talk about it.
They are already spending something on Meta, since we can see five live ads. If spend is tiny or paused, the plan and budget talk changes.
They can supply or approve honest creative that matches their site (honey, 45 calories) rather than the current '0 sugar 0 calories' claim.
The 14 reviews on the Variety Pack reflect it being the most-bought product. If Chris says otherwise, we swap the lead product.

questions.question: What are you spending on Meta right now, and roughly what does a month look like for you there?
questions.listening_for: A real daily or monthly number. This tells you if $150 a day is a step up, a step down, or about right. If they say almost nothing, this is a launch, not a fix.

questions.question: Who is running the ads today, you, Jack, or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is an incumbent agency to replace, an in-house person, or nobody. Also who really signs off. Chris is Head of Marketing but Jack is the founder.

questions.question: When you look at an order, what is a typical basket, one pack, a subscription, or a few packs at once?
questions.listening_for: Their real average order value. Everything on the site is $32.99, but the $16.50 subscription price could pull the real number down. This decides if the margin can carry paid ads.

questions.question: On a 12-pack at $32.99, roughly how much is left after the cost of the drink and shipping?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is enough room to pay for ads. Drinks are heavy and shipping eats margin. If margin is thin, we lean hard on subscriptions and repeat orders, not one-off sales.

questions.question: Last time you pushed ads harder, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Whether they have hit a wall before, and where. Past pain points tell you what they will be nervous about and what they will judge you on.

questions.question: I noticed two of your live ads say '0 sugar, 0 calories,' but the site says honey-sweetened and 45 calories. Which is right, and was that on purpose?
questions.listening_for: This is your opener that proves you actually looked. Listen for whether they know, whether it was a test, or whether it is a mistake. Either way it is a fast, honest win you can fix in week one.

questions.question: How many of your live ads are close copies of each other, and are they in the same campaign?
questions.listening_for: Confirmation of the duplicate problem. Two of their ads use word-for-word the same copy. If they sit in the same audience they are bidding against themselves and wasting budget.

questions.question: When someone lands from an ad, where do you send them, the product page or the homepage?
questions.listening_for: Whether the funnel leaks. If cold traffic hits a busy homepage instead of a clear product or Variety Pack page, that is a fix before we spend more.

questions.question: Which product actually sells the most, and which makes you the most money?
questions.listening_for: Confirm or correct the assumption that the Variety Pack is the hero. The review counts point that way, but only they know for sure.

questions.question: You have very few reviews showing on the site. Are you collecting them, and can we get customer photos or short clips?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is social proof to put in ads. Low review count hurts conversion. If they have reviews sitting unused, we can surface them fast.

questions.question: How is the Subscribe & Save doing, and how many people stick past the first month?
questions.listening_for: Whether repeat orders are strong. If people stay subscribed, we can afford to spend more to get the first order. If they churn after the $16.50 month, the model is shakier.

questions.question: Are you seeing people search for you by name or for 'hydration soda' after they see your ads?
questions.listening_for: Whether search demand exists. 'Hydration soda' is a term they are trying to own and similar drinks (like the big electrolyte and better-for-you brands) do get searched. If people search it, a small Google Search campaign to catch your own brand name and category is a real opening. If nobody searches it yet, say so and hold off.

questions.question: You have a Store Locator, so are you in retail too, and do you want online ads to help drive people in-store?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is a retail side. If they are in stores, local and search ads near those stores can help. This is a genuine opening, not a forced one.

questions.question: Is your blog or 'Science' content getting any traffic from Google today?
questions.listening_for: Whether SEO is worth raising. They have education pages ('What is a hydration soda'). If those already pull traffic, SEO is a real add. If not, do not push it during a 30 day paid test.

objections.objection: We are already running ads and they seem fine.
objections.answer: That is exactly why the fatigue report mattered. Two of your live ads use the same copy, so they are likely splitting budget against each other, and two of them make a '0 sugar 0 calories' claim your own site contradicts with honey and 45 calories. Fixing those two things costs nothing and should help before we even talk about spending more.

objections.objection: A $33 pack does not leave much room to pay for ads.
objections.answer: Fair, and that is why the first order is only step one. Your real money is the Subscribe & Save at $16.50 the first month and repeat orders after. If a good share of buyers stick, we can afford to spend to win that first sale. That is why I asked about your subscription numbers, so we size the budget to what the repeat orders can carry.

objections.objection: We have barely any reviews, will ads even work?
objections.answer: Low reviews do make cold ads harder, so we plan around it. We lead with the Variety Pack, which has your most reviews, and we build in a step to surface any customer photos or clips you already have. Getting more proof up is part of the first 30 days, not an afterthought.

objections.objection: Thirty days feels short to prove anything.
objections.answer: Agreed, and I am not promising a revenue jump in 30 days. The goal is narrower and honest: find the one angle and one audience that buys, and kill the ones that do not. That clarity is what lets us scale with confidence after, instead of guessing.

red_flags.flag: Live ads claim '0 sugar, 0 calories' while the site says the soda is honey-sweetened and 45 calories per can.
red_flags.why_it_matters: This is a direct contradiction. It confuses buyers, breaks trust, and false nutrient claims can get ads rejected or flagged. Raise it gently as something you can fix, not as an accusation.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible reviews (2 on Peach, 0 on Tropical, 14 on Variety) despite the site claiming '10,000+ Happy Customers.'
red_flags.why_it_matters: Thin social proof lowers conversion on cold traffic, and the gap between '10,000+ customers' and near-zero reviews is something a sharp buyer will notice. We need real proof in the ads.

red_flags.flag: All products are priced at $32.99 and the subscription first month is $16.50.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If most orders come in at the $16.50 subscription price, the money available to pay for a first sale is tight. Do not assume $33 orders. Confirm the real average order value before promising anything.

red_flags.flag: There are claims we must never make: burns fat, detox, boosts immunity, cures dehydration, 'healthiest soda,' or 'low sugar soda' as a headline.
red_flags.why_it_matters: These are off-limits for compliance and for the brand. Keep the creative to flavor-first, honey-sweetened, 45 calories, and everyday hydration. Do not let excitement on the call drift into a banned claim.

other_channel_openings: Google Search for their own brand name and the 'hydration soda' category, but only if Chris confirms people are actually searching it. Worth a small test to catch buyers who saw a Meta ad and later searched.
Local and search support tied to their Store Locator if they are in retail, to push people toward stores that carry Strive.

SEO on their existing 'Science' and 'What is a hydration soda' education pages, but only if those pages already pull Google traffic. Do not push this during the 30 day paid test if the demand is not there yet.
